title: Processing Your Sadness
description: The machine is trying very hard. It has been trying since before you were born.
idea: A fake system dialog box that is earnestly trying to defragment, compress, and optimize your sadness like it's a hard drive. Progress bars inch forward, tasks complete one by one (Locating Childhood Wound... Patching self-worth.dll...), and just when it's about to complete, an error occurs and it must start over. Infinite, sincere, and deeply unsettling.

plan.title: Processing Your Sadness
effects: Progress timer that advances and completes tasks
Error trigger near completion
Auto-restart after error
interactions: OK button on error that restarts
plan.visual_style: Windows 98/XP era dialog box aesthetic, centered on a dark desktop background
plan.implementation_notes: Use CSS to simulate old Windows dialog chrome. Animate progress bar with setInterval. Each task takes varying time. Error at ~95% completion.

title: You Have Been Selected
description: Congratulations. The envelope contains your name spelled backwards in gold. Please respond by Thursday of last week.
idea: The page opens with a formal congratulatory letter — gold seal, dignified serif font — announcing the user has won 'The Award.' But each time they click to accept or learn more, a new letter arrives that subtly contradicts the last: the awarding body has a different name, the prize has changed (a feeling? a specific Tuesday? 'all the medium-sized rocks'), and the tone grows increasingly frantic and needy, as if the organization desperately needs the user to accept this award or something terrible will happen to them. By the tenth letter the formatting is falling apart, the seal is just a potato drawing, and the organization is begging. The joke is that nothing is ever explained and accepting does nothing — the next letter just arrives anyway.

plan.title: You Have Been Selected
state_variables: letterIndex: number tracking which letter (0-9) is currently displayed
isTransitioning: boolean for letter swap animation state
acceptCount: number of times user has clicked accept (for subtle desperation escalation)
sealRotation: number for increasingly wonky seal rotation angle
effects: On letterIndex change, trigger brief fade-out/fade-in transition by toggling isTransitioning with a setTimeout
On mount, set a subtle paper texture background via body style, cleaned up on unmount
key_interactions: Click 'Accept This Honor' or 'Learn More' button — advances letterIndex, increments acceptCount, triggers transition
At letter 9 (final), button still works but loops back or shows a final broken state with the same desperate letter re-rendering with slight mutations
Seal element is clickable and wobbles/spins on click as a meaningless interaction
plan.visual_style: Starts as cream parchment with deep navy text, gold embossed seal, elegant serif font — progressively degrades to off-white with Comic Sans-adjacent inline font, red urgent text, skewed/rotated elements, a potato SVG replacing the seal, and increasingly large/bold desperate typography. Formal → unhinged.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Store all 10 letter configs in a static array of objects containing: orgName, awardName, prizeDescription, bodyText, tone (affects color/font/layout), sealType (SVG circle with initials vs inline potato SVG path), and buttonLabel. Derive visual degradation (font-family, border-style, color, transform skew) from letterIndex using interpolation so styles smoothly worsen. The seal should be an inline SVG that morphs from a crisp circular emblem to a lumpy potato outline using different SVG paths per stage. Use CSS transitions via inline style transitionProperty on the letter container div for the fade effect managed by isTransitioning state.

title: Translate This Feeling Into Livestock
description: A bureaucratic form from the Ministry of Approximate Emotions insists your feelings are measurable in goats.
idea: The user is presented with an official-looking government form titled 'Emotional Livestock Conversion Request (Form EL-7C).' They select a feeling from a dropdown (options include 'that specific dread when you remember an email from 2014,' 'the warmth of a stranger's sneeze,' and 'being perceived') and the form solemnly calculates how many goats, geese, or draft horses their feeling is equivalent to. The conversion logic is maddeningly specific — 3.7 goats, 'one goose with reservations,' 'a horse that is mostly regret.' Each result comes with official footnotes citing fake legislation and a stern warning that the Ministry does not accept appeals. A small stamp animates onto the page. Nothing is explained. The form resets and politely asks if you have another feeling to convert.

plan.title: Translate This Feeling Into Livestock
state_variables: selectedFeeling: string — currently chosen feeling from dropdown
result: object|null — { livestock: string, quantity: string, footnotes: string[], warning: string } after conversion
isCalculating: boolean — brief delay state to simulate bureaucratic processing
showStamp: boolean — triggers stamp animation after result appears
formKey: number — incremented to reset form smoothly
effects: When isCalculating becomes true, set a 1.8s timeout to compute result from a hardcoded lookup map, then set result and trigger showStamp
When showStamp becomes true, set a 300ms timeout to add 'stamped' visual state (rotation + opacity settle)
key_interactions: Dropdown onChange updates selectedFeeling and clears any previous result
Submit button triggers isCalculating state and shows processing indicator
After result displays with stamp animation, a 'Convert Another Feeling' button resets by incrementing formKey and clearing result/selectedFeeling
Stamp element animates from scale(2) opacity(0) rotated to scale(1) opacity(1) slightly rotated using CSS transition via inline style toggling
plan.visual_style: Aged government document aesthetic — off-white/cream background (#f5f0e8), dark navy text (#1a2340), serif fonts, thin borders, faded red stamp ink (#8b1a1a). Dense bureaucratic layout with form fields, section dividers, fine print. Slightly yellowed paper feel. Official seal placeholder using unicode/emoji. Austere, deadpan, institutional.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Store all feeling-to-livestock conversions as a hardcoded object map with keys matching dropdown values, each entry containing quantity string, livestock array, footnotes array citing fake Acts and Directives, and a stern Ministry warning — all authored for comic specificity. The stamp is an absolutely positioned div with text 'PROCESSED — MINISTRY OF APPROXIMATE EMOTIONS' rotated ~-15deg, rendered in red with border, toggling between pre/post animation inline styles via showStamp+stamped booleans. Use a formKey as the key prop on the form container to force clean remount on reset. The isCalculating state shows a blinking 'PROCESSING REQUEST...' message with ellipsis animation via a setInterval updating a dot-count state.

title: The Mouth That Knows Your Name
description: A vowel escaped from a dictionary and has been living behind your refrigerator. It recognizes you.
idea: A single large animated mouth dominates the screen, rendered in fleshy, slightly wrong colors, slowly moving and muttering to itself. When the user types anything — anything at all — the mouth reads it back phoneme by phoneme in increasingly inappropriate emotional registers: their grocery list with the gravity of a funeral oration, a random letter with the joy of a birth announcement, silence with existential accusation. The mouth has opinions about what you type and will editorialize ('that word again,' 'interesting choice,' 'we both know what you meant'). The longer the user interacts, the more the mouth seems to remember previous inputs and reference them obliquely. Nothing is stored. The mouth is lying. It just wants you to feel known.

plan.title: The Mouth That Knows Your Name
state_variables: inputText: current text the user is typing
mouthPhase: 'idle'|'speaking'|'muttering'|'accusing' — drives animation state
currentUtterance: the mouth's current editorial response string
emotionalRegister: 'funeral'|'birth'|'existential'|'suspicious'|'tender'|'offended' — cycles and escalates
interactionCount: number of times user has typed/submitted, drives 'memory' illusions
displayedPhonemes: array of characters being revealed one by one
phonemeIndex: current index in phoneme reveal sequence
mouthOpenAmount: 0-1 float controlling jaw animation
isBlinking: boolean for eye-like lid animation on mouth corners
previousWordHints: last 2-3 'remembered' word fragments used in editorials (fake memory)
effects: Idle mouth animation: oscillate mouthOpenAmount on a slow sine wave with slight irregularity using setInterval when mouthPhase is 'idle'
Phoneme reveal: when inputText changes, trigger character-by-character reveal via setInterval updating phonemeIndex, set mouthPhase to 'speaking'
Editorial generation: after phoneme reveal completes, pick editorial comment based on emotionalRegister + interactionCount, display as currentUtterance
Emotional escalation: every 3 interactions, shift emotionalRegister to next in escalation sequence
Muttering loop: when idle >4s, generate a random sotto voce mutter string and briefly animate mouth
Blink effect: random interval (3-8s) toggles isBlinking for 200ms to animate mouth corners drooping
key_interactions: User types in a hidden/minimal input field — each keystroke triggers mouth response
After brief pause in typing, mouth 'reads back' what was typed phoneme by phoneme with animation
Mouth editorializes after readback with register-appropriate comment referencing previousWordHints
Clicking the mouth itself triggers an accusatory response ('you touched me')
Pressing Enter submits and triggers the most dramatic emotional register response
Hovering near mouth causes subtle recoil/lean animation via mouse position tracking
plan.visual_style: Near-black background (#0a0806). Mouth rendered purely in CSS/divs — fleshy pinks and wet reds (#c97b7b, #8b2020, #f0c0b0), slightly desaturated and wrong. Lips slightly too large. Text responses appear in a pale, serif-like monospace below. Overall mood: intimate, wrong, uncomfortably aware.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Mouth shape built entirely from CSS border-radius div layers — upper lip, lower lip, teeth strip, dark throat — animated via inline style transforms and mouthOpenAmount state. Editorial comments stored as a large hardcoded lookup table keyed by emotionalRegister + interactionCount bucket, with template slots for previousWordHints to fake memory. Mouse position tracked via onMouseMove on container to drive subtle mouth-lean transform. All 'memory' is purely generative illusion — previousWordHints stores only 2 fragments from recent inputs to splice into pre-written editorial strings, making the mouth seem to recall without actually storing anything meaningful.

title: Obituaries for Abandoned Tabs
description: Each window held a small intention. The intention has since become a mineral.
idea: The screen presents itself as a solemnly designed memorial website for browser tabs the user never got around to reading. On load, it generates a fresh batch of deceased tabs — each with a fabricated title, a date opened, a date of abandonment, and a brief eulogy written in the sincere, florid style of a small-town newspaper obituary. 'Tab #4,891 — "How Long Can Shrimp Live On Land?" — opened Tuesday at 2:14am, survived by 47 of its siblings, never got the answer it deserved.' Users can click a small flower icon to 'pay respects,' which triggers a micro-animation of a pixel flower wilting onto the tab's card. A counter at the top silently increments, tracking how many tabs have died worldwide since the page loaded — a number that is completely fabricated and disturbingly large. The longer you stay, the more the eulogies start to sound like they're about you.

plan.title: Obituaries for Abandoned Tabs
state_variables: tabs: array of generated deceased tab objects (title, opened, abandoned, eulogy, respects count, wilting state)
worldwideDeaths: large fabricated counter incrementing over time
startTime: timestamp when page loaded for escalating personalization
wiltingTabs: Set of tab ids currently mid-wilt animation
respectsPaid: Set of tab ids user has already paid respects to
effects: On mount: generate 6-8 tab obituary objects using curated absurd-but-plausible title lists and procedural eulogy templates
setInterval every 47ms: increment worldwideDeaths by random 1-7 to feel disturbingly real
setInterval every 90s: mutate one random eulogy to subtly shift second-person, replacing 'the tab' with 'you' or 'they say you once'
key_interactions: Click flower icon on a card: add to wiltingTabs, trigger CSS keyframe-like inline style transition of flower drooping, increment that tab's respects count, add to respectsPaid set
Hover over a card: slightly brighten the card border, show a thin black mourning ribbon overlay effect
Scroll behavior: cards fade in sequentially with a somber downward drift entrance animation via useEffect + setTimeout stagger
plan.visual_style: Cream/ivory background (#f5f0e8), deep charcoal text (#1a1a1a), muted black borders, thin serif font feel via font-family Georgia. Sparse layout like a newspaper memorial section. Flower emoji as pixel stand-in. Header in small-caps. Overall mood: sincere, hushed, slightly uncanny.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Generate eulogies from a template engine that splices tab title, fake timestamps, sibling count, and cause-of-death phrases — with a personalization array that swaps in second-person language based on elapsed time since mount (tracked via Date.now() diff). Wilt animation is handled by toggling inline transform/opacity on a flower span via a timeout sequence stored in useRef. Tab titles should be drawn from a hand-curated pool of 40+ plausible-but-poignant queries ('Why do I keep doing this', 'Flights to somewhere', 'Is it too late to') to maximize the uncanny valley of recognition. The worldwide counter should start at a seeded large number (e.g. 847 million + offset) so it feels cosmically indifferent.

title: The Gravity of Your Last Gesture
description: A room full of objects that fell when you weren't looking. They are still falling. They want credit.
idea: The screen shows a sparse, infinite white room rendered in forced perspective. Objects — a lamp, a briefcase, a ceramic owl, a single shoe — are frozen mid-fall at various angles, suspended impossibly. When the user clicks any object, it continues falling in slow motion, rotating realistically, while a deadpan caption appears explaining WHY it fell ('This lamp fell because you left a room in 2019 without saying goodbye to it. It has been falling since.'). The longer it falls, the more the explanation becomes existentially personal and specific, as though the object genuinely resents the user. Objects can collide with each other mid-fall, spawning new falling objects with increasingly unhinged genealogies. Nothing ever lands. The floor is theoretical. A small counter in the corner reads 'Objects Currently Falling On Your Behalf' and only ever goes up.

plan.title: The Gravity of Your Last Gesture
state_variables: objects: array of object state {id, type, x, y, vx, vy, rotation, rotationSpeed, falling, fallDuration, caption, captionStage, scale, opacity}
fallingCount: total objects currently falling (only increments)
tick: animation frame counter for physics loop
activeCaption: {objectId, text, stage} for displayed caption
collisionQueue: pending collision-spawned objects to add next frame
effects: requestAnimationFrame loop: update y/rotation of all falling objects, detect pairwise collisions (distance threshold), spawn new objects on collision, increment tick
captionStage timer: every 4 seconds an actively-clicked object advances its captionStage, mutating caption text to more unhinged/personal content
on mount: initialize 6-8 static suspended objects scattered across the perspective room at varied sizes/angles
key_interactions: Click suspended object: set falling=true, begin slow vx/vy descent, show stage-0 deadpan caption, increment fallingCount
Click already-falling object: jump to next captionStage immediately, show more unhinged caption variant
Collision detection: when two falling objects come within threshold, spawn a new suspended object between them with a genealogy caption referencing both parents
Hover object: subtle wobble animation via rotation oscillation to suggest instability
plan.visual_style: Stark white background with faint perspective grid lines converging to a vanishing point. Objects drawn as simple emoji or Unicode glyphs at varied scales to fake depth. Captions appear in small serif italic below objects, dark charcoal, clinical tone. Counter top-left in monospace. Minimal, museum-quiet, slightly wrong.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Render objects as absolutely positioned divs with CSS transform rotate+translate, using scale to fake perspective depth (larger = closer). Physics uses simple Euler integration with very low gravity constant (~0.02) and slight horizontal drift for realism. Caption text stored as arrays of escalating strings per object type, indexed by captionStage (0-4+), generated from templates that become increasingly second-person accusatory. Collision uses Euclidean distance between object centers; spawned children inherit averaged position with random offset and combine parent captions into absurdist lineage strings.

title: The Bureau of Unfinished Gestures
description: A wave that never arrived. The hand remembers departing but cannot locate the destination shoulder.
idea: The screen presents a clinical government interface — 'PENDING GESTURE REGISTRY, Department of Incomplete Human Contact' — filled with a scrollable queue of gestures that were started but never finished: a hug that stopped at elbow-height in 2017, a high-five that was left hanging during a fiscal quarter, a tap on the shoulder that turned into a near-miss and a lifelong silence. Each entry is a form card with fields like GESTURE TYPE, INTENDED RECIPIENT (described only as 'a person near a refrigerator'), CURRENT STATUS ('hovering, 0.3 inches from contact, since March'), and a progress bar frozen at 94%. The user can click 'ATTEMPT COMPLETION' on any gesture, which triggers a slow, glitchy animation of two abstract limb-shapes lurching toward each other — then freezing again, just barely not touching, with a new bureaucratic error code: 'ERR_MOMENT_ELAPSED,' 'ERR_RECIPIENT_HAS_MOVED_ON,' 'ERR_THE_FEELING_CHANGED_SHAPE.' A new unfinished gesture is quietly added to the queue each time they try. The queue is described as 'current as of your last breath.'

plan.title: The Bureau of Unfinished Gestures
state_variables: gestures: array of gesture entry objects (id, type, recipient, status, progress, errorCode, attempting)
attemptingId: id of gesture currently being animated
errorVisible: bool for showing error code overlay
totalAttempts: count of completion attempts (for queue growth)
effects: On mount: initialize with 5-7 hardcoded gesture entries
When attemptingId set: run a 3-second timeout — animate limbs lurching via interpolated style, then freeze, set errorCode, add new gesture to queue, clear attemptingId
key_interactions: Click ATTEMPT COMPLETION: set attemptingId, trigger limb animation sequence
Scroll through gesture queue cards
Watch new gesture quietly append to bottom of queue after each failed attempt
Observe progress bar frozen at 87-96% per entry
Read error code that appears after animation freezes
plan.visual_style: Pale institutional gray-green background (#e8ebe6), monospace fonts throughout, muted olive and beige card faces, red-orange error codes, clinical form aesthetics — think DMV meets medical records, cold fluorescent mood
plan.key_implementation_notes: Each gesture card is a form-like div with labeled fields rendered as mock input rows (read-only). The limb animation uses two absolutely-positioned SVG arc/curve shapes (abstract arm silhouettes) whose translateX values are driven by a useEffect interval that increments then abruptly halts a few pixels apart. Error codes are drawn from a fixed pool of ERR_ strings and displayed in a modal-style overlay with a case number. The queue array is the single source of truth — new entries are prepended with a 'NEWLY FILED' badge and a timestamp derived from Date.now().

title: Competitive Listening Championship Finals
description: Forty-three sounds are waiting to be heard in the correct order. The sounds do not agree on what correct means.
idea: The user is presented with an official tournament bracket — THE WORLD COMPETITIVE LISTENING CHAMPIONSHIP, SEMIFINALS — in which they must judge which of two sounds 'wins' by clicking on it. The sounds are things like 'the sound a thought makes when you almost have it,' 'ice deciding to melt,' 'a very confident wrong answer,' and 'the specific frequency of being watched by a painting.' After each choice, the winning sound advances but mutates slightly — gaining traits from the loser, becoming a hybrid abomination. By the finals, the last remaining sound is a description so long and contradictory it wraps around the screen. The user is then awarded a certificate declaring them 'Certified Listener, Grade: Uncertain' with a wax seal that slowly drips off the page. The whole thing is formatted with the intense graphic design language of ESPN bracket sports — bold fonts, high contrast, team colors — applied with complete sincerity to sounds that cannot exist.

plan.title: Competitive Listening Championship Finals
state_variables: bracket: array of 8 first-round matchups with sound descriptions
currentRound: which round we're on (0=R1, 1=QF, 2=SF, 3=Final, 4=Certificate)
currentMatchup: index of current matchup being judged in this round
winners: array of sounds that have won each matchup so far
champion: the final mutated sound description string
sealDrip: number 0-100 controlling wax seal drip animation
resultFlash: null or 'left'/'right' for win flash animation
mutationLog: array of hybrid trait strings appended after each win
effects: useEffect on currentRound===4: start sealDrip interval incrementing from 0 to 100 over 4 seconds then clear
useEffect on resultFlash: clear flash after 600ms timeout
key_interactions: Click left or right sound to choose winner — triggers flash, merges descriptions, advances bracket
After both sounds in a matchup shown, clicking advances to next matchup or next round
On certificate screen, a 'PRINT CERTIFICATE' button triggers window.print() styled print view
Hovering a sound card shows subtle glow/pulse as if the sound is 'performing'
plan.visual_style: ESPN/sports broadcast aesthetic: near-black background (#0a0a0f), electric blue (#0057FF) and red (#CC0000) team colors, bold condensed uppercase typography, gold accent (#FFD700) for championship elements. Bracket lines in white. Certificate is cream (#F5F0E8) with red wax seal SVG. High contrast, intense, completely sincere.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Sounds array of 16 absurdist descriptions hard-coded; after each matchup the winner mutates by appending ' (now carrying traces of: [loser snippet])' building a Frankenstein description. Bracket rendered as flex columns with SVG connector lines between rounds. The champion description in finals uses fontSize scaling down as string grows, eventually wrapping in a marquee-like scrollable div. Wax seal is an inline SVG circle with a drip path whose height is animated via sealDrip state, eventually disappearing off screen.

title: Please Rate Your Existence
description: Five stars blink like nervous eyelids. The universe has left you a follow-up survey. It needs this for its records.
idea: The user is presented with a customer satisfaction survey — clean, corporate, utterly sincere — except the product being reviewed is their own existence, delivered by 'The Management.' Questions include 'How satisfied were you with the texture of Tuesday?' and 'Would you recommend being you to a friend?' with radio buttons ranging from 'Warmly' to 'With Reservations' to 'I would not do this to a friend.' Each answer triggers a brief, solemn automated response from the system ('Thank you. This feedback has been noted and will affect nothing.'). At the end, instead of a thank-you screen, a new survey begins — this one reviewing the experience of completing the first survey, which eventually reviews the experience of reviewing the experience, collapsing into a polite, pastel-colored infinite regress. The design is aggressively friendly: rounded corners, soft lavender, a progress bar that never quite reaches 100%, a mascot that is just a slightly worried oval.

plan.title: Please Rate Your Existence
state_variables: surveyDepth: number — which recursive layer of survey we're on (0=existence, 1=reviewing the survey, 2=reviewing the review, etc.)
currentQuestion: number — index of current question in active survey
selectedAnswer: string|null — currently selected radio option before confirming
systemResponse: string|null — the solemn automated response shown after answering
answers: array — collected answers for current survey
blinkState: boolean — toggles star blink animation
progressWobble: number — fake progress percentage that never hits 100
mascotWorry: number — 0-3 worry level affecting mascot expression
effects: setInterval every 1200ms toggling blinkState for star blink effect
When systemResponse is set, auto-clear it after 2500ms and advance to next question
When currentQuestion advances past last question, increment surveyDepth and reset currentQuestion to 0 after a brief delay
useEffect on currentQuestion/surveyDepth to update progressWobble to a value that asymptotically approaches but never exceeds 94
key_interactions: Clicking a radio option sets selectedAnswer and shows confirm button
Clicking confirm triggers solemn system response display and records answer
Stars blink independently via blinkState — each star uses a staggered opacity calculation
Mascot (SVG oval with eyes) shifts expression based on surveyDepth — more worried at deeper levels
Progress bar animates smoothly but caps at ~94% with a subtle jitter
plan.visual_style: Aggressively corporate-friendly: soft lavender (#E8E4F0) background, white card with heavy border-radius, muted purple (#7B68AA) accents, pale mint for system responses, pastel yellow stars, Helvetica/system-ui font, generous padding, everything slightly too cheerful
plan.key_implementation_notes: Define survey questions as an array of objects with question text and answer options — generate depth-aware questions dynamically for layers beyond 0 (e.g. 'How satisfied were you with your satisfaction rating?'). The mascot is a pure SVG inline element: an ellipse with two small circle eyes whose vertical position shifts upward as worry increases. System responses are drawn from a fixed pool of solemn non-sequiturs. surveyDepth caps display concerns but functionally loops forever — questions at depth 3+ are procedurally generated from templates to avoid hardcoding infinite content.

title: Sincere Apology from Your Furniture
description: The ottoman has been holding something in. The lamp witnessed everything. Wood remembers pressure differently than you do.
idea: The user's screen fills with a series of handwritten-style letters from their furniture, each one a deeply sincere, specific, and slightly accusatory apology for something the furniture did or witnessed. The couch apologizes for absorbing seventeen years of your posture and returning none of it. The chair by the door apologizes for how long it held your coat that one November. Each letter is animated to appear as if being typed in real time, with crossed-out words, second thoughts, and postscripts that spiral into existential furniture philosophy. The user can click 'Accept Apology' or 'I'm Not Ready' — accepting causes the furniture to immediately send a follow-up letter that is somehow worse, more detailed, more aware of you than any object should be. Rejecting causes a long, hurt silence, then a new letter from a different piece of furniture that has been watching the whole exchange.

plan.title: Sincere Apology from Your Furniture
state_variables: currentLetter: object with furniture name, letter text segments, and metadata
displayedText: string of text currently shown (for typewriter effect)
letterIndex: number tracking which letter in the sequence we're on
phase: enum of 'typing' | 'waiting' | 'silence' | 'followup'
charIndex: number for typewriter character position
crossouts: array of {start,end} ranges to render with strikethrough
postscripts: array of strings appended after main letter
silenceTimer: number counting seconds of hurt silence
letterHistory: array of past letter summaries shown as folded envelopes
isShaking: boolean for rejection hurt animation
effects: Typewriter effect: interval that increments charIndex through currentLetter text segments, pausing at punctuation, inserting crossout markers mid-word occasionally
Silence countdown: when phase==='silence', interval counts up silenceTimer display, then after 8-12 seconds triggers new furniture letter
On letterIndex change: reset displayedText, charIndex, phase to 'typing', select next furniture piece and generate its letter content from predefined pool
Postscript reveal: after main text finishes typing, sequentially type each postscript with a delay between them
key_interactions: Accept Apology button: triggers phase='followup', loads a worse more-detailed follow-up letter from same furniture, button only visible when phase==='waiting'
I'm Not Ready button: sets phase='silence', shows hurt silence UI with timer, eventually loads letter from new furniture that references the rejection
Click folded envelope in history: briefly shows a one-line summary of that letter in a tooltip
Hover over furniture name in letter header: shows a small ASCII drawing of that furniture piece
plan.visual_style: Aged cream/ivory background, ink-brown and faded sepia tones. Handwritten-style font via font-family serif with letter-spacing tweaks. Letters appear on slightly off-white paper cards with subtle drop shadow and faint ruled lines. Crossed-out text in red-brown. Postscripts in smaller, slantier style. Overall mood: melancholy domestic intimacy, like finding old letters in a drawer.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Store all furniture letters as a predefined array of objects with name, opening, body segments (some marked as crossouts), and postscript arrays — no fetch needed. Render the typewriter text by splitting into spans, applying strikethrough style to ranges tracked in crossouts state. The 'worse follow-up' letters should reference specific details from the first letter using template strings that pull from the same furniture object's metadata fields. Silence UI is just a centered italic counter with the furniture's name displayed dimly, creating dread through emptiness.

title: Vending Machine for Lost Futures
description: The coil turns. Something drops. You already owned this. You always did.
idea: A glowing vending machine UI occupies the screen, its glass display filled with spiraling coils holding small packaged futures — 'Architect You, Age 34,' 'The Version That Stayed,' 'Child You Would Have Named Morrow,' 'The Tuesday Where You Said Yes.' Each item has a price in a currency called NERVE, and the user starts with exactly enough for one purchase. Clicking a selection causes the coil to slowly rotate, the package to inch forward — then stop. A small paper receipt prints from the bottom: 'Item Unavailable: Purchased By Previous Self. See Claim Form K-9.' Every item, no matter which one, has already been bought. The machine apologizes in the flat affect of a broken appliance. But if the user waits long enough without clicking, the machine hums, flickers, and silently vends something unexpected into the tray — a small animated package labeled only 'THIS ONE' — which, when clicked, opens to reveal the current moment described back to them with absurd, specific tenderness.

plan.title: Vending Machine for Lost Futures
state_variables: selectedItem: index of currently selected/animating item or null
receiptVisible: boolean showing receipt at bottom
receiptText: string content of the printed receipt
coilRotation: number tracking rotation degrees for selected coil animation
machineFlicker: boolean triggering flicker CSS animation state
unexpectedItem: boolean — whether the mystery package has appeared in tray
unexpectedItemOpen: boolean — whether THIS ONE package has been clicked/opened
idleTimer: ref-tracked seconds since last interaction
nerveBalance: number starting at cost of one item
purchaseAttempted: boolean locks out further purchases after first attempt
effects: useEffect on mount: start idle countdown interval, increment idleSeconds every second
useEffect watching idleSeconds: at threshold (~18s), trigger machineFlicker then set unexpectedItem true after flicker sequence
useEffect watching selectedItem: animate coilRotation from 0 to ~270deg over 2s using requestAnimationFrame, then stop and show receipt
useEffect watching machineFlicker: toggle flicker boolean off after 1.5s
key_interactions: Hover over vending item: highlight slot with glow, show item name and NERVE price prominently

Click vending item: deduct NERVE, animate coil rotation, show item inching forward then stopping, print receipt with 'Item Unavailable: Purchased By Previous Self. See Claim Form K-9.' and machine apology in flat appliance tone
Click THIS ONE package in tray: open it with expand animation, reveal current-moment description with absurd tender specificity (time-aware text built from Date object)
Any interaction resets idle timer back to 0
plan.visual_style: Deep navy/black background with sickly warm fluorescent glow — amber and pale green. Machine body is dark gunmetal with scratched chrome trim. Items in coils are small pastel rectangles with tiny serif text. Receipt is white thermal paper strip. Overall mood: bureaucratic grief, vending machine existentialism, late-night convenience store melancholy.
plan.key_implementation_notes: The vending machine is a single tall centered div with a 'glass' panel (semi-transparent overlay with inner glow) containing a grid of coil+item slots rendered from a hardcoded items array. Coil animation uses a useRef for requestAnimationFrame, rotating an SVG spiral element. The receipt prints via a CSS height transition from 0 to auto on a white strip div at the machine bottom. The THIS ONE reveal generates its text dynamically using new Date() to inject real current hour, day, and season into a templated absurdist-tender description string.

title: The Dog Show for Invisible Dogs
description: Twelve leashes enter the ring. The judges weep without knowing why. Ribbon color: absence.
idea: The user is a judge at an intensely official dog show — full scorecards, dramatic announcer narration, applause sounds — except every dog is invisible. Each competitor is represented only by a taut leash held by a nervous owner, a small placard with the dog's name and breed ('Cornish Vapor Hound,' 'Standard Theoretical Retriever'), and a judging form with categories like PRESENCE, CONVICTION OF BEING, and COAT (IMPLIED). The user must score each invisible dog by clicking star ratings, and the system responds with total sincerity — crowd reactions, judge commentary, disqualifications for 'excessive non-existence.' The winner receives a rosette that floats in midair, held by nothing, trembling slightly in an unfelt breeze.

plan.title: The Dog Show for Invisible Dogs
state_variables: currentDogIndex: number — which of the 12 dogs is being judged
scores: object map of dogId -> {presence, conviction, coat, bearing, spectral_density} star ratings
phase: 'intro' | 'judging' | 'results' — show phase
announcerText: string — current dramatic narration line
crowdReaction: string — current crowd emotion descriptor
disqualified: Set of dogId strings
winner: dogId or null
rosetteTrembleAngle: number — for floating rosette animation
showScorecard: boolean — whether scorecard panel is open
completedDogs: Set of dogId strings
effects: useEffect on announcerText: auto-clear after 4 seconds back to idle narration
useEffect on currentDogIndex change: trigger entrance narration text with setTimeout cascade
useEffect interval: animate rosetteTrembleAngle with small sinusoidal oscillation when phase==='results'
useEffect on completedDogs size reaching 12: compute winner (highest total score, excluding disqualified), set phase to results, trigger final announcer sequence
key_interactions: Click star rating (1-5) for each of 5 categories on the judging scorecard — updates scores state immediately with crowd murmur text
Submit scorecard button: locks in scores for current dog, triggers announcer commentary based on total, advances to next dog
Disqualify button: adds dog to disqualified set with reason drawn from pool ('excessive non-existence', 'implausible coat', 'failed to not-bark convincingly')
Next Dog button: advances currentDogIndex with leash entrance animation
Click floating rosette in results: triggers brief trembling animation spike and a reverent announcer line
plan.visual_style: Deep navy and ivory — official, bureaucratic, slightly absurd. Gold accents for ribbons and stars. Serif fonts for placards and scorecards. The ring is a pale cream oval on dark floor. Leash drawn as a taut curved SVG line from owner silhouette to empty air. Atmosphere: hushed, reverent, genuinely emotional.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Each dog is defined as a static data array with name, breed (absurdist), owner name, and a flavor quirk used in announcer lines. The leash is an SVG cubic bezier with slight sway via CSS transform on a wrapper div using a keyframe-equivalent inline animation driven by a useEffect interval updating a 'leashSway' number in state. Star rating component is inline — five span elements that highlight on hover using a hovered index local state. Announcer text renders character-by-character using a typewriter effect driven by a useEffect with setInterval that increments a displayed-length counter.

title: Clouds Testify Against You
description: The sky has been subpoenaed. Each cumulus remembers exactly what you did at 3pm.
idea: The screen is a courtroom sky — pale overcast blue, with slowly drifting cloud-shapes that are the witnesses. Each cloud, when clicked, morphs into a vaguely accusatory shape and a stenographer's transcript appears below, recording the cloud's testimony about something the user did that it witnessed from above: a specific parking lot, a Wednesday, a face you made when no one was looking. The clouds speak in the clipped, emotionless language of court transcripts ('CLOUD EXHIBIT 7: The defendant paused at a crosswalk for eleven seconds and then continued. The motivation remains unclear.'). As more clouds are called to testify, a 'CASE STRENGTH' meter fills — but it's never clear what the case is about, who filed it, or what the verdict could possibly be. When the meter fills, the gavel falls and the judgment is read: something absurdly specific, solemnly delivered, and completely unactionable.

plan.title: Clouds Testify Against You
state_variables: clouds: array of cloud objects with id, x, y, speed, size, testified, exhibitNum, morphProgress
transcripts: array of testimony strings shown in stenographer panel
caseStrength: number 0-100 representing meter fill
verdictReached: boolean for when meter fills
verdictText: string for final judgment
gavelFalling: boolean for gavel animation state
exhibitCounter: number tracking total clouds called
effects: requestAnimationFrame loop to drift clouds horizontally, wrapping at screen edges, updating x positions
When verdictReached becomes true, trigger gavel animation then display verdict after short delay
On mount, initialize 6-8 clouds with randomized positions, speeds, and sizes
key_interactions: Click a cloud: trigger morph animation (morphProgress 0->1 over ~800ms), add testimony to transcript panel, increment caseStrength by 12-18, mark cloud as testified (changes opacity/shape)
Testified clouds still drift but are visually 'spent' — dimmer, slightly distorted shape
When caseStrength>=100: gavel SVG animates downward, verdict text fades in over courtroom sky
Transcript panel auto-scrolls to latest entry
plan.visual_style: Pale overcast blue (#c8d8e8 to #dde8f0 gradient sky). Clouds are white/grey SVG blob shapes. Transcript panel is cream (#f5f0e8) with monospace courier font, thin black border. Case strength meter is a thin horizontal bar in dark navy. Gavel is simple SVG. Mood: bureaucratic dread, absurdist solemnity.
plan.key_implementation_notes: Clouds rendered as SVG with multiple overlapping ellipses; on testimony trigger, morph by animating ellipse rx/ry values toward a more angular/accusatory arrangement using interpolation in the rAF loop. Pre-write a pool of 20+ testimony strings referencing hyper-specific mundane moments (parking lots, Wednesdays, micro-expressions) and verdict strings. Each cloud's morphProgress drives a CSS-style transform via inline style on the SVG group. Transcript panel is a fixed-height scrollable div positioned at bottom of screen.
